headings: THIS BELONGS A PAGE BACK AT LUMBIER
pages.text:
(Simon dropped the 3 of us off at the municipal swimming pool around the corner, while he drove to the supermarket. We expected a lift back, & it was only when Charles decided to walk, 1½ hours later, that we realised Simon had already got back. The cool swimming was marvellous (10 min along road).

__THIS BELONGS A PAGE BACK AT LUMBIER__

We had bought white asparagus (tinned) which Charles said looked like wax candles, it didn't taste very good. The cider was acidic. I offered to put it in the French dressing. The cheese is rather like parmesan. The jar of tuna fish in olive oil was very good. Lake Yesa.
__Thursday August 5th__
	Sunny all day. We are staying on at Ruesta. In the morning we read & John beat Eve at Scrabble. At 3.30. we set off for the lake again. John & Eve swam across to the promontory. Simon didn't enjoy the swim[...]

pages.sketch_desc: A hand-drawn outline shape near the top of the page, appearing to represent a lake or geographic feature (possibly Lake Yesa or the local lake mentioned in the text). No directional arrows or labelled place names within the sketch itself.
pages.text:
Our campsite has been levelled & lots of poplar trees planted.

[sketch of lake or terrain outline]

lots of old cong[illegible] haired [?hippies] at the site - relaxed but cl[illegible]

because the clay/sand was so slimy. Cha[rles] played with the yellow boat. John & Charles got cross because Eve wouldn't let them cross the lake - John swimming & Charles in the boat.
	We returned to the campsite & walk[ed] up to the nearly-deserted town of Ruesta for supper. There was a pilgrims' supper at 8.30. which cost us £8 each - bacon soup, plaice & salad & an orange. Charles refused to eat fish, so we have had all sorts of 'Plaice jokes' since then. The pilgrims are Spanish, French & German. Simon weeded one of the yellow arrows on the track by our campsite because pilgrims were missing it.

Friday August 6
	There was endless drumming in the night, everytime any of us woke up. There is also a range rover next to us with an English couple in a tent. During the night they had a noisy row with the man shouting that his wife was FAT. He, himself, had a terrific paunch & was much fatter than she was!
	John & Eve set off at 9, carrying 1 Mas bar, a bottle of water, the boat & pump. John swam 2k + across the lake. It took 1hr 5min. Simon & Charles packed up camp & drove to the bridge on the far side. They flashed us with the washing-up tray, to tell us where to come in.
	The middle of the lake was very beautiful & peaceful - a turquoise colour, with a wind mill farm far in the distance to the left, & the Pyrennes, that look like clouds, on the right.
	We drove on to Jaca & saw lovely capitals - David & Musicians & Salen's Ass at the

Cathederal. We found the restaurant - Hotel Oznas - & had our best meal in Spain - a glass of gazpacho, cold meats (there was also tuna & potato stew) & liver & chips, then chocolate mousse for pud (not good). Then excellent coffee.
	We drove east & found a level park for tea. There is a man doing a rush seat, maybe a Gypsy.
	We (Simon & Eve) had wanted to give the last campsite 5 stars, but John & Charles would only let us give 4 - there were only cold showers & lots of flies, no drinking water & the lake had slimy mud in the bottom. Charles got laughed at for being English (he thinks they still have spite because of the Armada).
	We travelled over a high pass & came down through Aragon. There were at least 5 deserted villages. We finally reached Ainsa (John: 'this wine Ainsa bad!') & shopped. The new town is down by the river & the

captions: Alto Aragón
pages.text:
Alto Aragón

old town is up on the hill. Charles swam. [?Ainsa] *** (J+C?) **** (S+E) is across the river from the town. There is a long campsite / lovely pool, lots of showers (warm

+ very- well-designed shower cubicles. Eve washed clothes + J, C+E hung them on the line at 8.30.pm. I had spent the 30min while the machine washed chopping the salad. After hanging the clothes we had supper. ($3.80 token from camp supermarket).
		During the night I was woked by a flash, then lots of flashes + rumbles. Charles + Eve dashed out to get in the clothes (nearly dry). Simon saw to the tent + John wondered what was going on.
		__Friday August 8th__
		We've had a lazy morning, playing bridge + swimming. During lunch + during the afternoon nap it) rained. Tea at 4.30.
		There is a huge mix of campers - Catalan Spanish campers with children, 2 Dutch guys in a tent + Porche, German (?) bikers - very disciplined, arrived in leathers & put up 2 tents straight away in blazing heat, a biker with a tent + 4
				pedal

captions: hilltop → old town view from campsite this side of river
Ainsa
pages.text:
hilltop
→ old town view from campsite this side of river

Ainsa

sensible 4 men tents & green marquee of a group of English Scouts, complete with Union Jack. This afternoon they got clothes bag

out of their van - probably with Scout uniforms in. (Jamboree?) There is a cafe/bar at the campsite, but we are going to walk into the town this afternoon (5.30) to sightsee & try the restaurant mentioned in the guide book.

When we arrived at the campsite we were nearly out of water - we had used Bham water until then. Simon filled with the hose near the entrance .... the water tasted disgusting & chlorinated, so we bought drinking bottles from the shop. After a day, Simon saw more hoses round the field ... he realised we had filled with swimming pool water, so we emptied & re-filled.

It has been marvellous swimming in the heat & washing my hair in a warm shower afterwards, once at noon, once at 5.30. I've put olive oil on my hair because it's sodry, which has made it difficult to wash now

At 6.30. we set off along a footpath through the fields to the river. There's a raised canal for watering lush allotment gardens. All the plants are

pages.sketch_desc: Hand-drawn diagram (not a geographic map) showing a bellows mechanism with three numbered positions. Labels include 'Salmon on wall.' and 'Fixed middle Froth'. Arrows and circles indicate the movement of parts. This is a mechanical sketch, not a route map.
pages.text:
twice the size of English ones. We used the road bridge to cross the river into the modern town. I suggested we could catch a bus up to the old city (it was still tremendously hot) + was met with a cold stare + "Is that a joke". So we launched up a very steep flight of steps + arrived at cobbled streets. Very pretty but a bit gnomy in parts. Big square, little squares
	We went in a museum of country crafts: basket weaving, horseshoeing, an __enormous__ __bellows__ for the blacksmith.
5ft high ?

[hand-drawn diagram of bellows mechanism with labels: "Salmon on wall.", "Fixed middle Froth", and numbered annotations]

① Man pulls chain which raises bottom chamber to push air out + [?raises/collapses] top chamber at ③ to [?send] air [illegible]. [?out]
② Man releases chain [illegible] which [?surpasses/pumps] air from top chamber [illegible]
③ + lowers chain to [?sink/send] air into bottom chamber.

weaving wool smocks, carding wool, spinning, wood carving – spoons, dough troughs, table-top wooden containers with sliding lids, donkey saddles, nail-making in black smith forges, [?rattle] children's walkers.) Saw pit saws.
	The cathedral had a terrific barrel vault, like the restaurant. Lovely cloisters with a display of the Way of St. James, [strikethrough: but a] tiny, but a Spanish girl chatting at full volume on her mob phone. For $1 you could have 5 min of a flood lit church + Gregorian Chant, which cut-off in mid bar. The crypt had low-down capitols, which made them easy to see.
	The restaurants only opened at 8.30, although we were ready for food at 8. I sat on a terrace + watched forked lightening strike the mountains in the distance. Sparks seemed to fly where the forks hit the rock. It was very beautiful.
	The supper was delicious. John + Charles had yet more gazpacho + Simon + Eve had well-boar ravioli + truffle sauce. Then Charles had rabbit, quail + chicken, John had sea bream + Simon + Eve shared paella – mussels + scampi – the works. Then there was lemon pudding –

We walked out into a brewing thunderstorm & sheltered in 2 doorways. 2 men stood in Simon's doorway & one smoked his way through the storm. (Even at the scout jamboree in the square there were men in uniform smoking). The rain poured down in stair rods & thunder & lightning crashed simultaneously, like a hammer horror film. When it eased up we walked back to the campsite down the steps & along the road because it was too dark to see in the fields.

__Sunday August 8th__
    John went swimming yesterday with 5 of Charles' MDs in his pocket! Apparently they all still work! Simon & Charles are furious at the campsite owners who won't exchange other goods from the shop for our enormous drinking water bottles, that we no longer need.

    We set off eastward & went up into a dead-end valley, Gistaín, to find a church. The service in Plan village fortunately started 35 minutes after we got there. It was a lovely, full church, a small prayer area with cushions at the back, too

captions: Plan Church, Valley of Gistaín, Aragon Pyrenees
pages.text: altars with flowers, one had a book open with lives of the saints. The congregation sang the service with enthusiasm. The service, with sermon, took 35 minutes.

captions: Bodegas de Sobrarbe (supper in Ainsa)
pages.text:
1      We went back down the valley (no swimming because of a damn) & had lunch down a narrow track (nerve-wracking because of the drop alongside into the river. We all swam in the bluey water. It was freezing. We had lunch.

Bodegas de Sobrarbe (supper in Ainsa) .-
(The Barrel (stone) vaulted roof was typical of church interiors, too.)
   It was a long drive on over the border into France, through a tunnel going upwards, Tunnel of Bielsa. Then on down into France in first gear

by   We reached a French Village, __Massat__ * * *. a municipal campsite. Clean warm showers but no pool. We got the last pitch & were so close to the next campers that we smelled their cigarettes all the time. Walking around the village after supper was nice. Good views of a mountain valley.
   __Monday__
   We left for the enormous cave of Bédeilhac . It is so big that an aeroplane flew out of it. During the war the Germans built an aeroplane factory in it. The 1½ hour guided tour was rather energetic, looking at red dots & [?bison] bison, paintings in red, yellow & black & carvings & gougings. All in French.
   We had no bread for lunch so we drove on to a Park of Prehistory, which we very

captions: Copies (of Niaux Cave paintings) in [?Prehisto], Salon Noir [?bisons]. www.sesta.fr Taraskon sur Ariège
pages.text:
Copies (of Niaux Cave paintings) in [?Prehisto], Salon Noir [?bisons]. www.sesta.fr Taraskon sur Ariège

much enjoyed. It was good to have the drawings I made clearer through infra red camera removing 15,000 years encrustations I had not realised before that they used natural curves in rock face too. An astounding

reproduction of 5, 7+13 year olds! footprints in sand of cave. Carvings of reindeer in bone. An English commentary through head phones. We're off to buy bread. We bought myrtle pie (blackcurrant or mulberry?) + drove on to le Malageou Camping *** near Ax-les-Thermes. There was a terrific thunder storm with heavy rain in the night. Very warm.
__Tuesday August 10th 2004__
We are camped next to a river which has space set aside for fishermen in wheelchairs. Crowded campsite but quiet + clean. The woman used a computer to give us a printed out map showing [?] pitches still unused "(" + we could choose our own + then go back + tell her the number. A local market at the campsite - I bought honey (10 k. away, rhododendron, so not local!) + goats cheeses - she had photos of her goats. Last night John beat Eve at scrabble with Clippers over a red - 83 points.

We stopped at Ax to shop, a lovely big market, cheese, olives [Ve...]
cake, apricots, honey baskets. An Andean music group-
Simon bought 2 CDs, one for goddaughter, Rosie.
, Lots of thermal spas here. A king built one for
returning Crusaders who had leprosy.
	We drove on to Montaillou. It's a
small, attractive village, parts decayed, parts
repaired. The Cathars Bookshop was closed, Old [th...]

Very well organised museum with workshops for children
springs on the hillside. Simon, Eve & Charles
walked up to the castle ruin (John went
off for a pee + failed to catch up with us)
There were 2 families having a picnic at
the foot of the castle. Donkey [saddles?] were

on the grass beside them + the donkeys were grazing nearby.
	After lunch we set off to drive over 2 hours to Cassalls' Prades. The drive was amazing - heavily wooded [so] narrow valleys, hardly anyone living there, hydro electric dams. We came out into Col de Jau - views unfortunately clouded, but 5 very naughty horses terrorising car drivers for bread!! We ate our rock cakes in the van for protection, + drove off.
	We went past Spa hotels in Moltig. Then we reached Prades. The Municipal Camping was complet. We have driven on to the Abbaye St. Michel de Cuxa (also on the Pilgrim's Santiagoway.) Charles went in + found a programme + Simon telephoned to book tickets for a concert on Thursday. On to try to find a campsite.
	Reached Les Santerelles ** [Filbls?], high up with views, (sadly overgrown orchard) swimming pool, good showers, but £15 a night, whereas last one £30.

captions: Les Pyrénées-Orientales - Vernet-les-Bains - [Chorale du Bain / church building]
pages.text:
John + Charles say it's only 4½ star!
Wednesday August 11th
	Pain au chocolat + croissants for breakfast were stale. Simon telephoned to reserve place for another concert, but fully booked.

[Vertical marginalia, left side, rotated:] Vernet les Bains was a very fashionable resort for "taking the waters", especially in about 1900. Some of the best hotels were swept away by a flood in 1940. Another [?] showed the [?Baptist?] Church.

We had a lovely relaxed day at this campsite, which is one of the nicest we have ever been to. Lots of shade. Huggledy juggledy. John beat Eve at Scrabble in the morning Eve beat John in the evening. Charles practised shuffling cards. In the afternoon the boys swam while Simon & Eve walked around the campsite. It's pretty huge - 68 sites at least. 2 more wash places. One shower takes wheelchairs, another is reserved for children. The owners seem to like children. They have lots of puzzle games & books in the bar. In one of the books I found "myrtles" means blue berries. I also learned about chestnuts & silk works.
	On our walk we also found a little stone room with a stone domed roof, big enough for 8 people to squat in, made in a terraced wall, hidden.

We swam, had tea & drove into __Vornet__ to look around & have supper. Eve bought a sun dress for Flora in Australia. Simon bought wine & food. John bought a wasp trap.
	We traced where the river had changed course in 1940. The cafe where we had supper was pleasant, out on the square, but one harassed man was doing all the work. There were 2 men with hunchbacks & I wondered if they were coming to take the waters. One of them was English. We went inside the Portugese Hotel & walked in the park. Elegant.
__Thursday 12th August__
	We got up earlier than usual & just managed to walk up the steep slopes to [?St. Martin's] Abbey in the shade. Any later & walkers were toasted. Charles stayed at the van (9.30 - 11.45.) The way was up roads used by 4x4 Landrovers driving people to the top. Lots of chestnut &

headings: Abbey of St. Martin of Canigou
captions: Saint-Martin du Canigou
Pays Catalan
pages.text:
11 + 12  Abbey of St. Martin of Canigou

[postcard: Saint-Martin du Canigou / Pays Catalan]

olive trees for shade near the top. Canigou is almost on the same longitude as Paris, so was marked on maps in Louis XIV's time. 2700m.
   The Abbey is gorgeous, restored in [20?] + used as a retreat house with Benedictines.

captions: ABBAYE SAINT-MARTIN-DU-CANIGOU
Carving of incense bearer in funeral of an abbot at St. Martin's at Canigou
pages.text:
When the last monks left in 1783 (the last 5 asked to leave) the abbey was looted by local people + capitals used to decorate private houses. The [20 circled] bishop persuaded people to give them back. It has been beautifully restored. I liked it alot. Lovely church interior, good lighting & peaceful, eve with [?Tourists]

[postcard: ABBAYE SAINT-MARTIN-DU-CANIGOU — carving of two stone figures]

Carving of incense bearer in funeral of an abbot at St. Martin's at Canigou

pages.sketch_desc: Small hand-drawn sketch of the stone room / truffle hut seen from above, showing a domed/oval structure with outer walls. A note labels it 'from above' and another note reads 'little walls to keepin pigs'.
pages.text:
We returned to the campsite, Swam + had lunch. Charles had so much sun yesterday that today he is wearing a T shirt + hat.
  John beat Eve 305-152 at Scrabble with "revisit" on a red. Eve had to give up. The campsite owner told us where to find another stone "room" - a 10 minute walk down the hill. He said they were built 200 years ago for shelter from the rain for the guardians of the truffles. This one was magnificent. About 12 feet high in the domed centre, windows, massive blocks of stone, windows, all made like drystone walls. Covered in ivy.
[sketch of stone room structure from above, with label "from above" and note "- little walls to keepin pigs"]
We returned to the campsite + played cards.
  I get very nervous that we may go over the edge on these windy roads. Charles says that I shouldn't get anxious, but if Thomas gets nervous, then I should be nervous + if Peter gets nervous, then I should get very, very

nervous.
  At 7 we set off to __Cuxa Abbey__ for the concert that we had booked.
  ... The concert was from 9-11.45., on hard seats. A man recited French poems with a piano accompaniment for ages - about a thunderstorm, a blind singer + a battle? - none of which we could understand, which was a pity. Charles got bored + went back to sleep in the van.
  However, parts of the concert were excellent, 'cello playing, sextet (which we may have heard in B'ham, Tillach quartet, + viola + 'cello, from Czech) playing Dvorak [strikethrough: At Hjorgat]. There was also scraping modern music. During the interval we bought juice from a family selling it in the courtyard, apricot juice + almond juice.
__Friday August 13th__
  We said goodbye + drove about 25 mins to Eux, a village on a steep hillside. One of the modern buildings was holding the

students' end of course concert. It was super- very enthusiastic + lovely pieces. The students could communicate so well, inspite of speaking different languages.

Very few English people.

11 am - 1.15.

last piece

[printed programme glued in:]
Franz Schubert
Quintette en mib majeur op.44
4ème et 5ème mouvement
Violon, Marta Deak (Hongroise)
Alto, V. Naguy Zsuzsanna
Violoncelle, Sébastien Renaud (France)
Contrebasse, Renske Lambert (Pays-Bas)
Piano, Danijel Detoni (Croatie)

We drove on trying to find a supermarket to buy food for lunch.

We ended up watching our pizzas being made at Ille-sur-Tet. We had to eat them sitting in the van, not the square, because they were not cut into slices. John had large, Simon & Charles had medium, Eve had small.

By 5, we still hadn't found a super market, but Simon is going to the campsite first to drop Eve off to wash clothes + try to get sheets dry before bed.

The washing dried incredibly quickly at Trebe*. Apart from that the campsite is pretty dire. Although there was a pool, John refused to swim, even if he could borrow a skimpy swim suit - trunks were not allowed. Add [John crossed out] Simon & Charles could have used Simons, but after a 2 hour shopping trip, they were too tired.

Our pitch was [illegible] right on the corner, outside the normal pitches, in the bend of the road, where people piss + pooh their dogs, so we were camped on alot of shit. The playground was just across from us with manic children throwing stones + riding bikes at each other. [He crossed out] It was a relief to leave.

__Saturday, 14th August__
	We drove into __Carcasson__, a fortified town, which was once taken by Simon de Montfort. The walls were originally Gallo-Roman, the usual square stones with red bricks, but in about 1300, the builders put new, massive blocks of stone above & below the earlier construction, to strengthen it. The towers are this [oval shape drawing] shape, for strength, & entrances are limited & overlooked by lots of arrow slits.
	The church has fabulous stained glass, trees of Jesse, & Life & 2 magnificent rose windows, lovely statues & a big square carving (sarcophagus) of The Siege of a city — Crusader helmets & an angel. The Seig Stone
	Gorgeous shops & I bought a 3m. tablecloth with blue grapes.
	We had lunch next to the Canal du Midi — quite small, that [struck] links Med. wi. Atlanti[c]

captions: Carcasson Aude
pages.text:
[Glued-in aerial postcard photo of Carcassonne with caption on postcard: Carcasson Aude]
↑ this area between the 2 walls was used for trying in fighting & jousting

As we go north, the hills are covered in woods.
The GAS computer map is useful. Simon can choose a carpark in a town & plot it on

then Charles can map read as to it .., though it can't anticipate height Barriers. The barrier was a 2m. pole at Carcassonne, but fortunately it was not in use.
    We left & drove north on small roads. Vingards. We failed to find a camp site with a ~~river~~ swimming pool, & it turned out that although __Réalmont__ ** had a river, it was green. It's revolting that people don't clean up dog shit. An English man was camping with a tent for a week while he let out his house the other side of the hill.
Sunday August 15th
    No bread for sale at the campsite, so we set off breakfast less. We stopped at a village & Charles bought croissants, pain au chocolat, baguettes & an apple pie to die for. We breakfasted in a lay-by.
    At times there is constant bickering between John & Charles ... like indigestion. The breakfast bicker was "why had Charles taken away the

captions: St. Cecily (B) Albi Cathedral.
pages.text:
tooth paste when John needed it ... again. Apparently Charles has to keep the paste with him to brush his teeth twice.... once isn't enough.
    On to Albi.

[photograph: St. Cecily (B) Albi Cathedral.]

captions: Gardens of Archbishop's palace, Albi, above R. Tarn
pages.text:
Gardens of Archbishop's palace, Albi, above R. Tarn

As the guide book said, the [?Cathedral] Truly huge, built of [illegible] brick. We went to church there. I also joined a guided group for a bit that was going around the cathedra[l]. Having totally crushed the Albigensian here[sy] in about 1244, the Catholics built this

Cathedral as a showpiece to prove they were back in power. The carvings are magnificent — Burgundian influence in heavy folds, very expressive faces & hands — Job's sad face & Judith's red dress. The Last Judgement [painting] covers the west wall — wrecked by having an organ sunk on top. Every inch of the interior painted. A large carved screen cut off the choir, so in 1980's they changed front to back & worship at west end. The new enamel altar cross stand out well. A good, enthusias[tic] service — feast of assumption of Virgin Mary

   We had lunch across the road. The archbishop's palace housed Toulouse-Lautrec's posters & sketches. He was amazingly sk[i]l[l]ful as a painter at only 15. He died of drink & his mother donated his work to the museum.

   Looking out the museum window was super — this very pretty garden

captions: Toulouse-lautrec museum, Albi. Profile of 12. by Villard. Woman putting on her stocking la Goulue at the Moulin Rouge Aristide Bruant in his cabaret. museum (Palaced the Bérbie) In the Salon of Rue des Moulins
pages.text:
Toulouse-lautrec museum, Albi. Profile of 12. by Villard. Woman putting on her stocking la Goulue at the Moulin Rouge Aristide Bruant in his cabaret. museum (Palaced the Bérbie) In the Salon of Rue des Moulins

We walked along the walkway* on the right by the river after seeing the museum. Then at 3 we set off to Eygmoutiers — a long drive, very hot & sweaty. When we got there at 8.30., we had trouble finding

captions: Henri de Toulouse-Lautrec par lui-même, 1896
pages.text:
the campsite Pierrette, [?]. It's on a large lake. It's very disappointing that, having wanted to [struck] swim for days & being much too hot, it rained in the night.
__Monday August 16th__
We started breakfast outdoors & had to finish it in the van. Simon & Eve walked up to the central building - ping pong, snooker & a library & newspapers. In the evening a BBQ. But the boys wanted to drive on, so we left. (They preferred to save time for the Normandy beaches than sit by a damp lake).

We bought bread in a town & kept driving north, having lunch in a lay by. (David's favourite rest. closed Monday)
At 5.30 we turned into __Futuroscope__ , the French scientific theme park. We stayed in 'Clarion' Hotel, which was quite good. The pool was small - we swam but

captions: Lou Mountognol, Pave de Chevre, Fromage au lait cru, Saveur du Rouergue
pages.text:
Not Simon. Then we walk[ed] 700m up the road (poor pavements & crossings for pedestrians) into the park. It was quite difficult to work out what to see - things close in the evening - & they forgot to tell us to pick up translation headphones at the entrance
    We saw 2 Imax (life on a space-craft & Icarus - making Leonardo da V's parachute). which by now is quite an old-fashioned medium - it's been around for 10 years.
    Then we saw coloured fountains with holograms - a pretty girl & a fish, which were pretty & impressive - (10 - 11.30).
    The park was well laid out with green grass & flowers.
    We slept in the hotel & left at 10 next morning, through queues to get into the park.
    __Tues August 17th__
    John knew the hotel was OK because there was a porsche boxster outside

later.... "A fiat bravo - who still drives a car like that ... a humping, dumping car..." - John near a camp site.
    We drove north, stopping for food. We had lunch at the entrance to a field where the considerate farmer avoided spraying us. We drove past Le Mans.
    At 5.45 we arrived at __Rouvrou__ in the Le Suisse Normand. Simon remember where it was. Unfortunately, just as we arrived, so did the rain. The campsite is [near] the bend of a river [on the] campsite. After the rain, we play[ed] [at the] statue of St. Joan. [near the] bridge. Then we went for a walk to look at the statue of St. Joan & the cemetery up on the hill. We returned to the van & rain pelted down again, just as John went off to the loo.
    __Wednesday August 18th__
    Mixed weather as we left. The church & house next door had a big walled garden. After reading the

captions: Les Chemins de la Liberté / The Paths to Freedom
pages.text:
history of the church, we realised that the village had bought the priory chapel to be its parish church – the walls must have originally enclosed the priory.
    We drove up towards the Normandy Beaches.

[postcard glued in: image of a dramatic sky/sea scene with the caption "Les Chemins de la Liberté / The Paths to Freedom" and the following poem printed on it:]
If it rains so often here
Is it to wipe out the blood
That soldiers shed
A long time ago …
[Union Jack flag symbol]
S'il pleut chez nous si souvent
Est-ce pour effacer le sang
Que des soldats ont versé
Il y a longtemps …
[French tricolor flag symbol]

The museum at Pegasus Bridge [?now] has an outside section with a Bailey Bridge + gliders. The Bailey Bridge was taken down in 1946 + used as a bridge for a local town over their river for 50 years. Then they built a new one + reassembled the [?Bhat] [?worst war office] the museum. Daphne du M. chose the name – her [?husb].
    We drove on to Arromanches + looked at the circular movie of the invasion + remains. Then had an ice cream.
    We arrived in __Bayeux__ + the campsite was 'complet' but Simon managed to talk his way in saying we were a camping van 'tres compact' + that we didn't need electricity. We walked into Bayeux + had a delicious supper near the Cathedral, but they took ages to bring the bill.
    Tremendous wind in the night. Charles was enveloped in the tent a couple of times. (The tent has a rip from old age + we hadn't put guy-ropes) + it hailed pine cones on top of the van.

captions: William the Conqueror & his half-brother Bishop Odo, after they arrived at Pevensey [?March] The bishop had fish because it's a Friday
pages.text:
John's birthday August 19th
We drove to the Tapestry. It was marvellous.
The headphones guide at the end took us through
at just the right pace. We saw the
cathedral.
     We stopped at Port en Bessin to buy

[photo of the Bayeux Tapestry — caption text along right margin, rotated:]
William the Conqueror & his half-brother
Bishop Odo, after they arrived at Pevensey
[?March] The bishop had fish because it's a Friday

John's birthday cake. Unlike the ribbon
development further East, it's a nice small
town.
     On to __Longues__. We had lunch at
the carpark & looked at the German gun
emplacements & a look-out post. It
could see all the way from Cherbourg
almost to __Pegasus Bridge__, but no
radar. & telephone wires cut by
bombing.
     __Pointe du Hoc__ was the last war
place we visited. The Rangers took it with
tremendous losses. There are craters all over
the ground — the land around is scrubby &
barren, so farmers haven't put the soil
together to farm again.
     We passed a German war cemetery.
There are 5 stone crosses to each plot &
stone squares in the grass. Charles said that
unlike the Allies, they share stones. 40,000
German soldiers died in this area.
     We went to [he] Clerk supermarket.
Simon bought lots of wine. We got gas
[h.p.] St. Low

On the way north we passed a lorry probably taking nuclear waste to England. It had 2 police cars behind + 2 in front with 2 policemen in each car. We also passed police cars in a layby. I persuaded Charles not to use his camera.

   We are also passed Freedom Stones - large white stones that mark every kilometre between Cherbourg & the Belgium Border.

   We reached __Barfleur__ (tide out) 4 boats on their side) at 8.30. The campsite looked pretty full. We filled up with water + parked for the night in the harbourside.

   We had a delicious supper to mark John's birthday. Iles flottant. (tide in) John Bottom but August 20th) Friday Charles top.

__August 20th) Friday__

   There was much technology at breakfast to find out Charles' RE result, GCSE. Simon's [run] contract had ended. John's phone ran out of battery. So they moved John's sim card into Simon's phone. But no record of William's number. (William was ringing Malvern yesterday to get the result). So Simon went back to his phone where William's number is stored.... +

after all that we got through, only to find that the results are not out for another week....

   9.40 am. glorious sunshine, tide coming in, but so are purply grey clouds.

   Simon + Eve walked around the harbour, jetty, church. It's a lovely village/town, with a Sailing school. The Vikings used to come here. Simon bought a pudding for supper - when all of us will be home at 86.

   We left at 10.45. The Cherbourg Ferry is 12.30.

   The ferry crossing was quite rough. We hoped to reach the Kennels before closing time, to get Tonic. But all the roads seemed to be jam-packed with cars.

   We finally reached home at 7. Thomas + Peter were there. William arrived later for the weekend. The boys gave Grandma, Grandpa & Helen a bbq the next day.

captions: John's 17th birthday.
Barfleur (postcard — aerial view of Barfleur harbour)
pages.text: John's 17th birthday.

Sunday 29th August.
        9.45. we set off, Simon, Eve, John, Charles, Melody 14 & Charlotte 13, to __Bath__ to Camp with David. We arrived & found their van at the Marina Campsite [illegible] but they had gone out. The 4 children played 4 in a Row & chess at the table, while Simon & Eve went out for a walk by the river. When we got back Eve had to yell "John, John Kirby!" several times before Charles came & let us in. Everyone was laughing.
        We had a very slow start to lunch with extremely strong cider provided by David & just as we started to carve the chicken at 2 pm the heavens opened with 2 heavy rain showers. We left the food under a parasol & sheltered in the vans. Finally we got fed.
        Soon after, at [?3].45. (!) we set off walking into Bath. Charles had hurt his back falling off the swing in the playground.

captions: Gorgon's head. Romano Celtic Head from Pediment of the Temple of Sulis Minerva, Roman Baths, Bath.
pages.text:
Gorgon's head.
Romano Celtic Head from Pediment of the Temple of Sulis Minerva, Roman Baths, Bath.

"The David Kirby were going to Sarah's 50"
birthday party. We all walked to see the

Roman Baths, which we enjoyed. Melody & Charlotte hadn't heard much about the Romans.

   We had a filling Turkish supper at Marmaris & caught 2 taxis back to the campsite.

      __Monday 30th August__

   We all slept well & ate breakfast outside. Dylan & Mia slept in after the party. We had a leisurely breakfast. Then it rained. So the children continued their 4 in a row & chess & cards in Simon's van. The adults chatted & drank coffee in David's van. We had lunch & went blackberrying. We left at 3.15.

      __Saturday 16th October__

   At about 11.45. we set off for Trimpley & then the farm. Thomas had arrived an hour earlier from America (Ohio). Simon, Eve, Thomas, Melody & Charlotte & Tonie visited Trimpley. Jim is very ill. We had sandwiches for lunch &

took it in turns to go up + see Jim in bed.
    On To the farm to see the apples. We walked through the orchards. Toni's got lost & came home alone. A beautiful day. We had been going to take Kate + Jannie out for a meal, but John was playing "The Swan" at Little Malvern Court. So we went to hear him. It was very posh - black tie - & John hadn't told us so I was in blue jeans, which was a bit embarrassing. £20 per ticket. A lovely old cruck barn. A picture of Laud's ([strikethrough]Abbot[/strikethrough] Archbishop Laud - Glory to God & Laud to the Devil) Laud from his cell blessing Stafford on his way to the block. An abbot & a nun. Carthusians. The woman was there whose family had had the house since the Reformation. Both Simon & I were worried her pashmina would catch fire as she put on logs. A glorious go oak or lime carved altarpiece.

John & Alice Howick & Greg played well, John playing the Swan. Unfortunately people were talking across them all the time. They only sat down to listen to a soprano & poetry reader.
    __Sunday__
    Eve taught Charlotte & Melody how to row. We went on for ages, so long that Kate ended up peeling the potatoes. She did a wonderful roast pork lunch. Charlotte & Oliver were there too. Then we made cucumber sandwiches for Charlotte's harvest festival tea.
    The girls rode up & down on the bike (that was the best part of their weekend.) We walked out to see where the archaeologists had been finding Roman Pottery remains on top of the hill. We made sandwiches & left for John's concert at Malvern.
    Charles came & sat with us. It was a good concert. Even the flute sounded good.
    __Monday October 22nd.__
    John, Melody, Charlotte, Simon & Eve

INFORMAL CONCERT

Sunday 17th October 2004 : 7.30 p.m. St Edmund's Hall

Bacchanale
    Jonathan Hill (timpani)
    Ian Wright

Tell me Lovely Shepherd (from Solomon)
    Scott Mahoney (treble)

Andante
    Andrew McDade (tuba)
    Capuzzi

Andante
    Scott Mahoney (treble)
    Andrew McDade (tuba)
    Capuzzi

    Hanano Kawase (piano)
    C. Debussy

Italian Dance
    Simon Eaton (oboe)
    Madeleine Dring

    B. Molique

'Am Abend da es kühle war'
"Mache dich, mein Herze, rein' (from St. Matthew Passion)

    J.S. Bach

    Shostakovich

    Hector Leung (piano)
    J.S. Bach
went on a day trip to Hanbury Hall 1801 near Droitwich. It's very attractiv[e] with wrought iron gates + lovely views over Worcestershire, near the Malverns. We saw inside an ice house,

pages.sketch_desc: Hand-drawn cross-section diagram of an icehouse structure. Labels include: 'hatch to shovel ice + Snow' on the left side of the structure, 'passage to take out ice' with an arrow pointing to the right passage, and 'drain to ice pond' at the base with a curved arrow. The sketch shows a domed/rounded chamber with a hatch entrance on the left and a horizontal passage on the right.
pages.text:
the icehouse was 6m wide & high
     built of brick.

hatch                 passage to take out ice.
to shovel [diagram of icehouse structure]
ice +
Snow          drain to ice pond

There was also a mushroom house
orchard, orangerie.

captions: Hanbury Hall parterre. 2002, NT & Nick Moss
pages.text: Hanbury Hall parterre. 2002, NT & Nick Moss

April 9th  2005.
	 At 11 we set off for __Aston Cantlow__.
On the way we had to stop in a layby for the brakes to get sorted - for the 3rd year running they've wrong straight after the winter. After 30 minutes we drove on.
	Thomas has just come back from US & Rome. William has started a CFA course in London. Peter has bought an internet cafe in Shanghai. John has stays at home to revise for the IB. Charles has brought revision along for his GCSEs. (He'll be 16 next week)
Pope JP2 was [illegible] buried yesterday.
Prince Charles is marrying Camilla today.
	We parked in the campsite, surrounded by plastic houses, + walked along the footpath to the pub, The King's Head. One of the best pub meals ever. Charles ha[d] [continues overleaf]

beef sausage. Simon & Eve had 5hour lamb + pumpkin + thyme mash wi rosemary sauce. Too full for pud. Back to the van for hot drinks.
	We napped + walked around fields outside the village. Flat, lots of apple trees, very pleasant but cold. Lambs. Tonic keeps licking his paws. We had a cup of tea + read. Supper we had in the van because the pub was already fully booked.
	The campsite has lots of ducks + geese + a third Bellamy award. They recycle rubbish. The woman who showed us to the pitch has 2 children. She's lived here since she was 8.
	The church has a lovely Millenium Sampler showing types of Embroidery stitches - Bayeux, Black Work, [illegible] Tudor Gold work, Jacobean Stump Work, Victorian flowers... Simon + Eve spent the evening with Coats + Woolley hat [?]s on. V. cold.

__Sunday April 10th 2005__
By the end of the evening I was wearing a brown sweater, aran cardigan, sheepskin waistcoat + yellow outdoor jacket. I couldn't face getting undressed. We both slept (well) in our clothes. I even pulled my hood on at one point in the night.
	Charles had Tonic in the tent where he licked his paws for much of the night (cuts from stubble in the fields?)
	It's a bit warmer today. The loos + shower www.islandmeadowcaravanpark   are clean + there are 2 free hairdryers. It seems well run, you can order newspapers.
	Tonic + Charles stayed at the van, Charles got chased by a [?dead] duck after feeding it bonio. Simon + Eve went to church, a nice service. (2 people were billed £95,000 by the PCC because they had bought rectoral lands.)
		We drove on to __Kinwarton Dovecot__

pages.sketch_desc: Two pencil sketches at top of page: left sketch shows a dovecote building (house-like structure with triangular roof and diamond/triangle window); right sketch shows a tall vertical structure with horizontal ladder-like rungs, apparently illustrating the interior nesting box arrangement of the dovecote. No route arrows or place labels on the sketches.
pages.text:
+ had lunch. [illegible] [illegible] [illegible] [illegible]

[pencil sketch of dovecot building and nesting box structure]

					[?potence] for
			reaching 580 nesting
			boxes.

Central pole pivoted, leaving ladder on 2 arms.   (14).
Dovecotes, columbanus, orig. brought by Romans. Court cases because doves fed for free on peasants crops. Messy floor v.g. fertilizer.
	We got home at 3.05. Sunday

__Friday May 27th__
	Simon + Eve left at 12.40. + then had to go back, at Lordswood Rd, for the name of Grandma's + Helen's b. + b.
		We are going to pick them up + take them to Malvern.
